From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

New Zealand geographer

| Karen Nairn | |
| --- | --- |
| Nairn in 2008 | |

| Academic background | |
| [Alma mater](/wiki/Alma_mater "Alma mater") | [University of Canterbury](/wiki/University_of_Canterbury "University of Canterbury"), [University of Waikato](/wiki/University_of_Waikato "University of Waikato") |
| [Theses](/wiki/Thesis "Thesis") | * [*Quiet students in geography classrooms*](https://hdl.handle.net/10092/104862) (1994) * [*Disciplining identities: gender, geography and the culture of fieldtrips*](https://hdl.handle.net/10289/15389) (1998) |

|  | |

**Karen Marie Nairn** is a New Zealand academic, and is a full professor of education at the [University of Otago](/wiki/University_of_Otago "University of Otago"), specialising in youth-centred research.

## Academic career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karen_Nairn&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Academic career")]

Nairn was a high school geography teacher interested in environmental issues, before entering academia.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Nairn completed a Master of Arts in geography at the [University of Canterbury](/wiki/University_of_Canterbury "University of Canterbury") in 1994, and then went on to do a [PhD](/wiki/PhD "PhD") titled *Disciplining identities: gender, geography and the culture of fieldtrips* at the [University of Waikato](/wiki/University_of_Waikato "University of Waikato").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) Nairn then joined the faculty of the University of Otago, rising to associate professor in 2014 and full professor in 2022.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

Nairn's research focus is young people and social movements. Nairn was the lead researcher on a 2017 [Marsden grant](/wiki/Marsden_grant "Marsden grant") about young people's engagement in [social movements](/wiki/Social_movement "Social movement"), with collaborators [Joanna Kidman](/wiki/Joanna_Kidman "Joanna Kidman"), Judith Sligo, Carisa R. Showden, and Kyle R. Matthews.[[6]](#cite_note-6) This work led to the publication of the book *Fierce Hope: Youth Activism in Aotearoa* in 2022, which covers youth-led groups working in areas such as indigenous land rights, sexual violence and social inequality.[[7]](#cite_note-7) This was the second Marsden grant Nairn has received, having published *Children of Rogernomics: A Neoliberal Generation Leaves School* in 2012 from an earlier Marsden-funded research project on the impact of [neoliberal reform](/wiki/Rogernomics "Rogernomics") in New Zealand.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) Nairn has also researched the impact of a Year 12 (last year of high school) leadership programme for young women.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

Nairn spoke about youth activism and [Ihumātao](/wiki/Ihum%C4%81tao "Ihumātao") alongside [Qiane Matata-Sipu](/wiki/Qiane_Matata-Sipu "Qiane Matata-Sipu") at the 2023 [Dunedin Writers and Readers Festival](/wiki/Dunedin_Writers_%26_Readers_Festival "Dunedin Writers & Readers Festival").[[8]](#cite_note-8)
